---
title: "Acuity"
subtitle: "Opinionated Quarto template inspired by Edward Tufte's principles"
author: "Ruslan Basyrov"
format:
  acuity-html:
    default
  acuity-typst:
    keep-typ: true
bibliography: bibliography.bib
---

## Abstract

{{< lipsum 1 >}}

Test sidenote^[The tested sidenote is here.].

# Font

The template uses a single font with high legibility and readability: Atkinson Hyperlegible Next (@brailleinstituteAtkinsonHyperlegibleFont).


# Color Palette

[Paul Tol's color palette](https://sronpersonalpages.nl/~pault/), designed to be visible by color-blind people, is used to provide sensible defaults with high contrast:

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| fig-align: "center"

# Implicitly read the palette from _brand.yml
brand <- yaml::read_yaml("_brand.yml")
pal <- unlist(brand$color$palette)

# Prepare the data for a grid layout
n_cols <- 4
df <- data.frame(
  name = names(pal),
  hex = pal,
  # Calculate grid positions
  x = (seq_along(pal) - 1) %% n_cols,
  y = -((seq_along(pal) - 1) %/% n_cols) 
)

# A compact grid visualization
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y, fill = hex)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = sprintf("%s \n %s", name, hex)),
    # Dynamically calculate brightness to make text black or white
    color = ifelse(colSums(col2rgb(df$hex)) > 500, "black", "white"),
    fontface = "bold",
    size = 3.5
  ) +
  scale_fill_identity() +
  theme_void() +
  theme(plot.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5))
```

# Semantic Color Testing

This section tests how Quarto maps semantic colors from `_brand.yaml` to standard document elements like callouts.

::: {.callout-note}
**Info Color:** cyan / `#88CCEE`.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
**Success Color:** green / `{{< brand color green >}}`.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
**Warning Color:** sand / `#DDCC77`.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
**Danger Color:** wine / `#882255`.
:::
